run.output:
Query returned 10 rows
Execution time: 3621 ms

Q3.notes: Baseline correctness verified. Performance is 9.4x slower than DuckDB (91.7ms) and 9.4x slower than previous GenDB baseline (386ms)

Q3.bottleneck: Single-threaded execution on a 64-core Xeon. The query processes 59.9M lineitem rows + 15M orders rows + 1.5M customer rows sequentially without parallelism. No SIMD vectorization.
Q3.root_cause: Complete absence of thread parallelism and SIMD. The code uses three sequential hash table scans: (1) Filter customer by mktsegment, (2) Hash join orders with filtered customer, (3) Hash join lineitem with filtered orders. Each pass is single-threaded. The aggregation phase uses std::map (O(n log n) insertion) instead of hash aggregation, and final sort is not parallelized or optimized for Top-10.

analysis.overall: Execution time of 3621ms is critically slow. The baseline was 386ms (GenDB's previous run), and DuckDB achieves 91.7ms on the same workload. With 64 cores, single-threaded execution wastes ~98.4% of available compute. The dominant bottleneck is the absence of thread parallelism — the workload involves scanning 75M+ rows and performing joins/aggregations that are trivially parallelizable. Secondary bottlenecks: (1) std::map for aggregation is O(n log n); should be unordered_map hash aggregation. (2) No Top-K optimization — full sort when only 10 rows needed. (3) No SIMD for filtering or aggregation. (4) Redundant dictionary lookups during customer filter.

critical_fixes.issue: Complete absence of thread parallelism on a 64-core machine, resulting in 40x slowdown vs DuckDB target
critical_fixes.fix: Implement morsel-driven thread parallelism: divide each table into fixed-size morsels (~100K rows) and use a thread pool to process morsels in parallel. Apply thread-local aggregation with final merge.
critical_fixes.description: The query pipeline must be parallelized: (1) Customer filter: partition customer table into morsels, each thread filters independently, building thread-local sets of matching custkeys. (2) Orders join: partition orders into morsels, each thread probes customer_custkey_map (shared read-only) and builds thread-local orders_map. Merge all thread-local maps. (3) Lineitem join: partition lineitem into morsels, each thread probes merged orders_map and aggregates into thread-local hash tables (keyed by {orderkey, orderdate, shippriority}). Final merge phase combines all thread-local aggregates. This enables near-linear 50-60x speedup on 64 cores.

critical_fixes.issue: Aggregation uses std::map (red-black tree, O(n log n)) instead of hash table, severely limiting aggregation speed
critical_fixes.fix: Replace std::map<GroupKey, double> aggregates with std::unordered_map<GroupKey_Hash, double> using a fast hash function and custom hash combiner for the composite key {orderkey, orderdate, shippriority}
critical_fixes.description: Lines 182-195: Change from `std::map<GroupKey, double> aggregates` to `std::unordered_map<size_t, double>` where the key is a fast hash of {orderkey, orderdate, shippriority}. Define a custom hash function that combines the three int32_t values (e.g., via FNV-1a or xxHash). This reduces insertion from O(log n) to O(1) average. Estimated improvement: 3-5x faster aggregation for 2M unique groups.

critical_fixes.issue: Full sort on 2M+ groups to extract Top-10 is O(n log n) and processes 200x more data than needed
critical_fixes.fix: Replace full sort with a min-heap-based Top-K selection that maintains only the top 10 results during iteration
critical_fixes.description: Lines 203-218: Instead of sorting all result_groups and taking first 10, use a std::priority_queue<ResultRow> (min-heap on revenue DESC) with size limit 10. This is O(n log k) where k=10, reducing sort cost from O(2M log 2M) to O(2M log 10). Also, move this to happen during lineitem iteration (maintain Top-10 incrementally) to avoid materialization of 2M intermediate results.

performance_optimizations.description: Implement morsel-driven thread-level parallelism across all three phases: customer filtering, orders join, lineitem join. Divide each table into 100K-row morsels. Use std::thread pool with atomic work counter to distribute morsels. Each thread processes its morsels independently, maintaining thread-local data structures (customer_custkey_sets, orders_maps, aggregates). Final merge phase combines partial results. Recommended thread count: 32-48 (leaving some cores for OS and other processes).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 40-45x speedup (approaching theoretical 50-60x on 64 cores, accounting for merge overhead and load imbalance). Expected time: 3621ms / 45 ≈ 80ms, competitive with DuckDB's 91.7ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace std::map with a high-performance hash table. Two options: (1) Write a minimal hash table using open addressing with linear probing, storing {hash_key, revenue_sum} pairs directly. Pre-allocate to 2.7M slots (75% load factor for 2M groups). (2) Use Abseil's SwissTable (folly::F14HashMap as fallback). For option 1: custom GroupKey hash function using FNV-1a: hash = offset_basis; hash ^= orderkey; hash *= FNV_prime; hash ^= orderdate; hash *= FNV_prime; hash ^= shippriority. This avoids object overhead of std::map nodes and improves cache locality.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-5x faster aggregation phase (O(1) vs O(log n) insertion). Combined with parallelism, contributes 0.5-1ms reduction.

performance_optimizations.description: Eliminate full sort of 2M groups for Top-10 result. Strategy: (1) During lineitem iteration, maintain a priority_queue<ResultRow> (min-heap) of size 10, keyed by {revenue DESC, orderdate ASC}. Push every aggregated result; if heap > 10, pop min. (2) Final step: extract 10 elements from heap (already sorted by revenue DESC, maintaining orderdate ordering). This reduces sort cost from O(n log n) to O(n log k) where k=10.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduces sort/materialization overhead by ~15-25ms (from ~30ms full sort to ~2-5ms Top-K heap ops).

performance_optimizations.description: Current code mmaps all columns but only uses a subset. Customer uses: c_custkey, c_mktsegment (2 columns). Orders uses: o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority (4 columns). Lineitem uses: l_orderkey, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_shipdate (4 columns). Skip mmapping unused columns (c_name, c_address, c_comment, etc.). Change MADV_SEQUENTIAL to MADV_RANDOM for hash probe tables (orders_map, aggregates) since access is random. For sequential scan phases, use MADV_SEQUENTIAL.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Save ~5-10% I/O time by avoiding unnecessary page faults on unused columns. With HDD storage, this compounds: unused lineitem columns (1.4GB l_shipinstruct + 475MB l_shipmode + 1.8GB l_comment = 3.7GB) should not be loaded.

performance_optimizations.description: Vectorize the customer filter (lines 143-147): instead of scalar loop `if (c_mktsegment_data[i] == building_code)`, use SIMD to compare 8-16 values per iteration. Similarly, vectorize the l_shipdate filter (line 187). Use AVX2 (256-bit SIMD, 8×int32_t comparisons per instruction) or AVX-512 (512-bit, 16×int32_t). This requires compact bit-vectors to collect matching indices, but reduces filtering latency.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 8-10x speedup on filtering loops (modest absolute gain since filtering is not the critical path with parallelism, but complements thread-level speedup). Roughly 2-5ms reduction.

performance_optimizations.description: The storage design includes l_shipdate_sorted.idx and metadata. This suggests zone maps may exist for l_shipdate blocks. If so, precompute min/max shipdate for each block and skip blocks where max_shipdate <= cutoff_shipdate (9204). This eliminates ~67% of lineitem I/O for blocks that don't satisfy l_shipdate > cutoff_shipdate. Current code doesn't use indexes; leverage them via a block-skipping wrapper.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Saves ~20-30% of lineitem I/O and computation by skipping ~2/3 of 60M rows at the block level. Estimated 200-300ms reduction when combined with parallelism.

summary: Q3 iteration 0 executes correctly but is critically underperforming: 3621ms vs. DuckDB's 91.7ms target (39x slower) and vs. the GenDB baseline of 386ms (9.4x slower). Root cause is complete absence of thread-level parallelism on a 64-core Xeon system. The single-threaded sequential implementation scans 75M+ rows across three joins without utilizing available cores. To close the gap, implement morsel-driven thread parallelism (Priority 1, expected 40-45x speedup to ~80ms), followed by hash aggregation optimization (Priority 2, 3-5x), Top-K optimization (Priority 3), and column pruning (Priority 4). SIMD and zone-map optimizations (Priorities 5-6) are secondary but valuable in combination. Estimated final performance after all optimizations: 15-20ms (2-6x better than current baseline, approaching DuckDB parity).
